claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 18 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and in approved education
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 18 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 17 and in approved education; Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is 17 and in approved education
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 18 and not in approved education or extension period
circumstances: Child is 18 and not in approved education or extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you check if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment for you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's begin the eligibility assessment.

**First, I need to know about the child you're claiming for:**

How old is the child?

- **Under 16 years old**
- **16 to 19 years old**
- **20 years old or over**

Please let me know which age category applies to your child.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi! Thanks for helping me with this. I actually have two children I'm wondering about - Alex who's 17, and Blake who's just turned 18. Should I tell you about them one at a time, or can we check both together?

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hi! You're very welcome, I'm happy to help you check eligibility for both Alex and Blake.

We can absolutely check both together - it will be more efficient that way. Since they're both in the 16-19 age range, the key factor will be whether they're in approved education or training.

Let me navigate to the next step for this age group:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
For **Alex (17)** and **Blake (18)**, I need to know about their current education or training status. For each child, please tell me:

**Are they:**
1. **Currently in approved education or training** (e.g., A-levels, T-levels, NVQs, Scottish Highers, apprenticeships, etc.)
2. **Recently left education or training** (within the last few months)
3. **Neither** - not in education/training and haven't been recently

You can tell me about both children at once, for example:
- "Alex is doing A-levels and Blake has just finished college"
- "Both are still in sixth form"

What's their situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Alex is still doing his A-levels at sixth form, so he's definitely still in education. But Blake finished college a few months back and isn't studying anymore - he's just been working and figuring out what to do next.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that clear information! Let me assess each child separately.

**For Alex (17, doing A-levels):** He's currently in education, so let me check the education level requirements.

**For Blake (18, finished college a few months ago):** Since Blake has left education recently, I need to check if there's any extension eligibility.

Let me navigate through both paths:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Good, I have the education criteria for Alex. Now let me also get information about Blake's extension eligibility:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
This is very helpful. Let me now assess both children:

---

## **Alex (17, doing A-levels at sixth form)**

A-levels are on the approved qualifications list. I just need to confirm a couple of things:

1. **Is Alex studying full-time?** (This means at least 12 hours per week of supervised study/lessons)
2. **Is Alex working alongside his studies?** If so, is it **less than 24 hours per week**?

---

## **Blake (18, finished college a few months ago)**

Unfortunately, the news isn't as good for Blake. Here's why:

- There is a **20-week extension** available for young people who leave education, **but** this extension only applies up until the child turns **18**
- Since Blake has already turned 18 and is no longer in education or training, the extension period wouldn't apply

This means Blake is unlikely to qualify for Child Benefit anymore.

---

**To confirm Alex's eligibility**, could you please let me know:
1. Is his A-level course full-time (12+ hours of lessons/supervised study per week)?
2. Is he working more than 24 hours a week alongside his studies?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Oh, I see about Blake - that's a shame, but thanks for explaining that clearly.

For Alex - yes, he's definitely full-time at sixth form, it's a proper full-time course with classes most days of the week. And no, he doesn't work at all actually - he's just focused on his studies right now.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's great, thank you for confirming those details about Alex.

So Alex is in full-time approved education (A-levels, 12+ hours per week) and isn't working at all - that's perfect. Let me continue through the assessment to check the remaining eligibility criteria.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good progress! A few more quick questions about Alex:

1. **Is Alex receiving any benefits in his own right?** (Such as Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit claimed by him personally - not benefits you claim for your household)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't receive any benefits in his own name - he's just a student living at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great! Since Alex is doing A-levels (not an apprenticeship), he passes that check too. Let me continue:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check your residency status. 

**Do you normally live in the UK?**

(If not, there are other ways to qualify - such as having settled or pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme, or being a Crown servant abroad - but living in the UK is the most common route.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - we're based in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great, Manchester counts! Now about your responsibility for Alex:

**Are you responsible for Alex?** This can be met if:
- **Alex lives with you**, OR
- **You contribute to his upkeep** (paying for things like food, clothes, pocket money, housing, etc.)

You mentioned earlier that Alex is "living at home with me" - so I assume he lives with you. Is that correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right - Alex lives at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Nearly there! 

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?** 

For example, does Alex's other parent or another family member claim Child Benefit for him? (Only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child at a time.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Just a couple more quick checks:

**Is Alex currently:**
- In hospital (for an extended stay)?
- In local authority care?
- Being fostered?

Or is he simply **living at home normally** with you in Manchester?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He's just living at home normally with me - nothing like that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Last question!

**Is the local council paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?**

(This would be things like the council paying for his housing or living costs directly - not general services like education.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, the council isn't paying anything towards Alex - we're just a normal family, I cover everything myself.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent news! I've now completed the assessment for both of your children. Let me give you the final outcome:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
---

## 🎉 Assessment Complete!

Here's your summary:

### ✅ **Alex (17) - ELIGIBLE**
Great news! Alex qualifies for Child Benefit because he:
- Is 17 and in **full-time approved education** (A-levels)
- Is not working 24+ hours per week
- Doesn't receive benefits in his own right
- Lives with you in the UK
- Has no other claimant and no council support

### ❌ **Blake (18) - NOT ELIGIBLE**
Unfortunately, Blake no longer qualifies because:
- He has finished education and is now 18
- The 20-week extension period (for young people who leave education) only applies **until the child turns 18**
- Since Blake is already 18 and not in education/training, he no longer meets the criteria

---

## **Next Steps for Alex:**

If you're not already claiming Child Benefit for Alex, here's what to do:

1. **Apply online** at [gov.uk/child-benefit](https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim) or by post using **form CH2**
2. **Provide** proof of identity and Alex's birth certificate
3. **Be aware** of the High Income Child Benefit Charge if you or a partner earns over £60,000/year
4. **Payments** are usually made every 4 weeks

---

Is there anything else you'd like me to clarify about Child Benefit for Alex, or do you have any other questions?

reasonings: Alex (17) is ELIGIBLE for Child Benefit. He meets all the requirements: (1) He is aged 16-19 and in approved full-time education (A-levels at sixth form, which is a non-advanced qualification with 12+ hours per week); (2) He is not working 24+ hours per week; (3) He does not receive benefits in his own right; (4) He is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship; (5) You (the claimant) live in the UK; (6) You are responsible for Alex as he lives with you; (7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for him; (8) He is living at home normally; (9) The local council is not paying towards his accommodation or maintenance.

Blake (18) is NOT ELIGIBLE for Child Benefit. Blake has finished college and is no longer in approved education or training. While there is a 20-week extension period available for young people who leave education, this extension only applies until the child turns 18. Since Blake has already turned 18 and is not in education or training, he no longer qualifies for Child Benefit.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The family has two children. Alex (17) qualifies for Child Benefit as he is in full-time approved education (A-levels) and meets all other eligibility criteria. Blake (18) does not qualify as he has left education and is over 18, meaning the extension period no longer applies. The claimant should continue or start claiming Child Benefit for Alex, but cannot claim for Blake.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.description: For 16-19 year olds, check if in education/training or eligible for extension
response.routing_logic: If in education/training, validate type and level. If just left education (within 20 weeks), check extension eligibility.

response.question: Is the child in approved (non-advanced) education or training?
response.help_text: Approved education: A-levels, T-levels, NVQ Level 3 or below, Scottish Highers/Advanced Highers. Advanced (NOT approved): University degrees, HNC, HND, NVQ Level 4+. Must be full-time (12+ hours/week supervised study).

criteria.must_not_be_married_unless_dependent: Married 16-19 year olds only qualify if financially dependent on claimant

response.question: Has the child (aged 16-17) left education within the last 20 weeks AND registered with a qualifying service?
response.help_text: 20-week extension available if child has left approved education/training and registered with: government careers service, Connexions, or armed services. Extension ends at week 20 or when child turns 18, whichever is sooner.

age_limits.birthday_rule: Benefit stops on child's 20th birthday, not end of academic year

training_exclusions: Training with employer agreement for future job

payment_rates.note: Current rates not specified in source documents - check gov.uk for latest rates

high_income_charge.note: High Income Child Benefit Charge applies if you or partner earns over £60,000. This is a payment adjustment, not an eligibility criterion.

response.question: Is the child in approved (non-advanced) education or training?
response.help_text: Approved education: A-levels, T-levels, NVQ Level 3 or below, Scottish Highers/Advanced Highers. Advanced (NOT approved): University degrees, HNC, HND, NVQ Level 4+. Must be full-time (12+ hours/week supervised study).

criteria.must_not_be_married_unless_dependent: Married 16-19 year olds only qualify if financially dependent on claimant

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.description: Applicant meets all criteria for Child Benefit
response.reason: Meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements
next_steps: Apply online or by post using form CH2
Provide proof of identity for yourself and the child
Provide child's birth certificate or adoption certificate
Be aware of High Income Child Benefit Charge if you or partner earns over £60,000
Payments made every 4 weeks (or weekly if single parent or receiving certain benefits)

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex (17) is ELIGIBLE for Child Benefit. He meets all the requirements: (1) He is aged 16-19 and in approved full-time education (A-levels at sixth form, which is a non-advanced qualification with 12+ hours per week); (2) He is not working 24+ hours per week; (3) He does not receive benefits in his own right; (4) He is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship; (5) You (the claimant) live in the UK; (6) You are responsible for Alex as he lives with you; (7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for him; (8) He is living at home normally; (9) The local council is not paying towards his accommodation or maintenance.

Blake (18) is NOT ELIGIBLE for Child Benefit. Blake has finished college and is no longer in approved education or training. While there is a 20-week extension period available for young people who leave education, this extension only applies until the child turns 18. Since Blake has already turned 18 and is not in education or training, he no longer qualifies for Child Benefit.

response.overall_reasoning: The family has two children. Alex (17) qualifies for Child Benefit as he is in full-time approved education (A-levels) and meets all other eligibility criteria. Blake (18) does not qualify as he has left education and is over 18, meaning the extension period no longer applies. The claimant should continue or start claiming Child Benefit for Alex, but cannot claim for Blake.
